text: Hello everyone and welcome to this lecture. In this lecture, we are going to cover a super powerful technique known as rag or retrieval augmented generation.
Let me show you what I mean by that. Of course the name looks pretty, I would say, complicated or fancy but don't worry, it's actually pretty straightforward.
So what is rag? Rag is a technique that combines retrieval based systems with generative AI models.
It's commonly used to enhance the capabilities of large language models or LLMs by integrating external knowledge sources.
Let me show you what I mean. Let's assume that you are a user here.
And what you're going to do is that you're going to pass along a prompt.
So you have chat GPT open, you send or write a prompt and that prompt is going to be sent here to a what we call it retriever.
So instead of taking that prompt and sending it directly to the brain or the artificial intelligence model or the LLM,
what I'm going to do first is I'm going to do a kind of think of it as an initial step before sending the prompt directly.
What I'm going to do is I'm going to retrieve a knowledge base. Think of it as let's assume that you are a medical company and you have let's say reports from previous diagnosis.
What you could do is that you can take this database and you can keep it and now you can send that prompt to what we call it a retriever.
The retriever is going to search the database that includes documents about let's say specific that to your company.
And that retriever is going to search the database and then it's going to fetch specific information.
So this is what we call it the first step in rag, which is the R term or retrieval.
Instead of taking a prompt, sending it directly to the brain, I'm going to check a database first.
That's the overall idea to look up that information.
So the R here means I'm going to look up the external source to retrieve the relevant information.
So instead of sending that directly to the model, now I can retrieve some of the information from my own specific database and now I'm going to perform augmentation.
Now I'm going to grab that prompt, the initial prompt that the user wrote plus context plus the retrieved information that I obtained from my database.
And that's the idea of the A here. It's called augmented because I'm going to take what the user wrote plus what I obtained from my database, augment them together, combine them together.
And this is the A here, which is add the retrieved information to the user prompt.
And now I can take that and send it to the LLM or large language model.
And this is the term generation. So now I'm going to generate basically an output using the augmented prompt plus context that I got from my database.
That's pretty much it. I would say for rag. And of course, rag has a ton of advantages.
So let me first show you from a very high level what is going on. So these are the steps for rag.
So again, this is the exact same diagram that I got before.
So what happens is first the user writes a prompt or a query that is passed to or to and what we call it an orchestrator.
What's going to happen is that orchestrator is going to send a search query to the retriever.
So that's what we got here. The part, the first part, which is the R part.
The retriever is going to fetch the relevant information from the knowledge source and send it back.
And then what it's going to do we are going to augment. So this is the A term.
The orchestrator is going to augment the prompt with the context and send it to the LLM.
Okay, so now I finished the R part. I finished the A part.
And now finally, the LLM is going to respond with the generated text, which is displayed to the user via the orchestrator.
And this is the last part, which is the generation part. And this is the idea of a rag in a nutshell.
So what are the benefits of doing rag? Well, there are so many benefits.
If you remember, in the previous lecture, I covered what we call it fine tuning.
So with fine tuning, we actually going to get the brain, which maybe have millions of trainable parameters.
I'm actually going to retrain the model. I need to calculate the gradient.
I need to go back. I need to update all the values of weights.
But what's going to happen here with rag, I don't really need to do that. Why?
Because the base model here is still the same. All I need to do is I need to integrate my custom base knowledge,
put it in a database somewhere. And now I can add that trick before I send my prompt directly to my LLM.
So first, it can allow you to generate more accurate responses.
So it combines retrieval with generative artificial intelligence models to ground outputs in factual data, minimizing errors.
Because I got or augmented the prompt with my own specific knowledge.
You can also include up-to-date information. So now I don't need to refer to the LLM directly.
Now I can include in the database maybe updated information. Maybe if I have new, let's say, medical records.
I can include that there. Maybe as a business, I can include let's say updated 10K reports, for example.
I can include that as well in the rag kind of database.
So it dynamically accesses the latest content, ensuring relevance without retraining.
It's also cost efficient. Now I don't need to retrain the LLM. I don't need to even touch it.
All I need to do right now is to update the database.
So that reduces computational burden by relying on retrieval for context, cutting costs instead of fine tuning,
which requires training of the base LLM.
It's also customizable. So it's easily tailored to specific industries and domains with custom data sets.
And finally, it's also explainable, meaning that it increases trust by surfacing the data sources behind generated responses.
So now I can explain basically why the LLM generated that output, because well, I contributed to that through my database that's implemented in the initial part, which is the retrieval part.
Okay, so the next question is, how does rag compare to fine tuning?
So when it comes to fine tuning, if you remember, what is it? Well, we are going to train the base model or the GPT on specific data sets to adapt to a particular use case.
And this is a fine tuning piece. But with rag, I'm going to combine the GPT's language generation with a retrieval mechanism that fetches external information.
How it works with fine tuning, I'm going to provide a labeled data sets that is tailored for a given task, and we're going to retrain the model using the data updating its weights.

We cover that many times as well in the past. But with rag, a document or a database is indexed using a vector database, and then we're going to send the query that is going to retrieve the most relevant chunks from that database.
And then the GPT generates responses by combining that it retrieved information along with the general knowledge from the LLM.
Okay, so what about use cases? So with fine tuning, it's useful for well structured, high quality data sets and niche understanding.

And of course, it requires computation resources and training expertise, right? You need to have or provision GPUs, you need to know how to run like a gradient descent, for example, algorithm, because you are actually fine tuning the weights of the model.
But with rag, it's ideal for application that requires up to date or domain specific knowledge without doing retraining.

When it comes to advantages, well, it deeply integrates the knowledge from the data sets, because we are actually think of it, think of it, now we have a brain, a digital brain, and you're actually changing the weights of that brain.
So if you are integrating that deep knowledge in the weights, all the information is embedded inside the brain with rag, think of it as just it's an ad hoc kind of operation.
The brain is still the same, we're just adding or augmenting it with our knowledge from a database. So it does not require retraining to the model and it integrates external knowledge.
And it's easier to maintain, so you can basically update that database without modifying the model. And of course, it's caused effective compared to fine tuning.

When it comes to the drawbacks or issues with fine tuning, well, it's expensive, it is time consuming, and a big issue as well is that it locks the model into a specific knowledge set, so it limits the flexibility for dynamic or broad queries.
However, for rag, first, it relies on the quality of the retrieval system and index data, and retrieval may fail if documents are poorly indexed or irrelevant data is fetched.
